sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Paediatric motor and mobility screening helps identify children whose movement, balance, coordination, or endurance suggests developmental delay, neurological disorder, injury, or need for formal paediatric rehab referral.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Delayed gross motor milestones or poor motor control
- Frequent falls, clumsiness, toe walking, or poor balance
- Difficulty running, jumping, stair use, or playground skills compared with peers
- Parent or school concern about endurance, coordination, or asymmetry

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Development is variable, but regression, asymmetry, pain, seizures, weakness, or bowel and bladder change require escalation
- Screening tools support judgement but do not replace a full developmental or neurological assessment
- Family concern can be clinically important even when the child compensates well in clinic

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Clarify what the child used to do, what they cannot do now, and whether there has been regression
- Screen safety, pain, asymmetry, and obvious neurological red flags
- Escalate urgent concerns rather than delaying for routine therapy referral

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Use age-appropriate screening of balance, transfers, gait, stairs, running, hopping, and play-based motor tasks
- Coordinate paediatric medical, neurological, orthopaedic, or developmental referral when delay or asymmetry is meaningful
- Guide parents on safety, play exposure, and follow-up expectations

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - In children, change over time and asymmetry often matter more than one isolated clinic performance
